_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against CDC FoodNet 2022, Crump NEJM 2015 review on bloodstream salmonella, IDSA 2017 guidelines on infectious diarrhea, and WHO global salmonellosis estimates.

h1Title: Salmonella Infection: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Salmonella infection (salmonellosis) is one of the most common causes of foodborne illness worldwide, with the CDC estimating 1.35 million US cases, 26,500 hospitalizations, and 420 deaths annually. The genus Salmonella contains over 2,500 serovars; non-typhoidal species (NTS) such as S. Enteritidis, S. Typhimurium, and S. Newport produce self-limited gastroenteritis 6 to 72 hours after exposure to contaminated poultry, eggs, produce, or reptile contact. Typhoidal serovars (S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi) cause typhoid fever, a distinct systemic illness covered separately. Most non-typhoidal cases resolve in 4-7 days without antibiotics. Severe disease — bacteremia, focal infection, or prolonged diarrhea — occurs in infants, adults over 65, and immunocompromised hosts, and warrants targeted antimicrobial therapy. Antimicrobial resistance is rising, with multidrug-resistant strains now detected in 6% of US isolates.

keyStats.prevalence: 1.35 million US cases per year (CDC); ~94 million global cases annually (Majowicz 2010 modelling)
keyStats.demographics: Highest rates in children under 5 (rate ~64 per 100,000) and adults over 65; men and women equally affected
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in infants and young children; second peak after age 65
keyStats.globalCases: ~94 million gastroenteritis cases globally per year; ~535,000 invasive non-typhoidal cases (NEJM 2015)

definition: Non-typhoidal salmonellosis (ICD-10: A02) is an intestinal and potentially invasive bacterial infection caused by Salmonella enterica serovars other than Typhi and Paratyphi. The organism is a motile gram-negative facultative anaerobe acquired primarily through ingestion of contaminated food (poultry, eggs, beef, produce, sprouts), contact with infected animals (reptiles, amphibians, backyard poultry, hedgehogs), or person-to-person fecal-oral spread. After ingestion of typically 10⁵ to 10⁸ organisms, the bacteria invade the small intestinal and colonic mucosa, enter M cells overlying Peyer patches, and trigger a neutrophilic inflammatory response with secretion-driven diarrhea. Most patients clear the infection by mucosal immunity within 4-7 days; roughly 5% develop transient bacteremia, and a smaller fraction (under 1% in healthy adults but up to 50% in HIV-infected children in sub-Saharan Africa) develop sustained invasive disease with focal complications including endocarditis, osteomyelitis, meningitis, and mycotic aneurysms. Asymptomatic stool shedding may persist for weeks after symptoms resolve, with implications for food handlers and childcare staff. The condition is managed by primary care for uncomplicated cases, with infectious disease and pediatric specialists involved in invasive, prolonged, or resistant infections.

typesClassification.description: The most common presentation: acute onset diarrhea, abdominal cramping, fever, and vomiting 6-72 hours after exposure, resolving in 4-7 days without antibiotics. Accounts for over 90% of non-typhoidal cases.

Transient or sustained bloodstream infection in 5% of NTS cases, more common in infants under 1 year, adults over 65, sickle cell disease, HIV, and chemotherapy-induced immunosuppression. Requires antibiotic treatment.

Salmonella-seeded osteomyelitis (classically in sickle cell disease), septic arthritis, meningitis (predominantly in infants), endocarditis, and mycotic aneurysm (especially of the abdominal aorta in older adults).
Distinct systemic illness caused by S. Typhi or S. Paratyphi; covered on the dedicated typhoid fever page. Differs in incubation, clinical course, and management.
Prolonged asymptomatic shedding of Salmonella in stool for more than one year, more common with biliary stones and typhoidal strains than with most non-typhoidal serovars.

primarySymptoms: Diarrhea — typically watery, sometimes containing visible mucus or blood, 6-72 hours after ingestion of contaminated food, lasting 4-7 days.
Abdominal cramping in the periumbilical or lower abdomen, often colicky and preceding each bowel movement.
Fever, usually 38-39°C, lasting 2-3 days; higher temperatures suggest invasive disease.
Nausea and vomiting in the first 24 hours, typically less severe than the diarrhea but contributing to dehydration risk.
Headache and myalgia accompanying the febrile prodrome, often resembling viral gastroenteritis.
Tenesmus and urgency with stool, particularly when colonic involvement predominates.
Hematochezia (visible blood in stool) in roughly 5-10% of cases, more common in pediatric infection.
Dehydration features in infants and elderly: dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output, lethargy, sunken eyes.
Joint pain (reactive arthritis) developing in roughly 2-15% of patients 1-3 weeks after the acute illness, especially in HLA-B27 carriers.
Bone pain or focal swelling in patients with sickle cell disease — concerning for salmonella osteomyelitis.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden-onset diarrhea and fever 6-72 hours after eating undercooked poultry, eggs, or recalled food product
Diarrhea in a person with recent reptile, amphibian, or backyard poultry contact
Cluster of household or social-group gastroenteritis after a shared meal
Diarrhea in a daycare or long-term care setting where a confirmed Salmonella case has been reported
emergencySigns: Fever above 39.5°C, persistent vomiting, or signs of severe dehydration (no urine for 8+ hours, altered consciousness) — possible invasive disease
Bloody diarrhea with severe abdominal pain — distinguish from hemorrhagic colitis caused by other pathogens
Newborn or infant under 3 months with fever and diarrhea — risk of bacteremia and meningitis; admit for evaluation
Bone pain, joint swelling, or focal back pain with fever in a sickle cell disease patient — possible salmonella osteomyelitis or septic arthritis
New back pain in an older adult with Salmonella bacteremia — exclude mycotic aortic aneurysm
Worsening symptoms after 5-7 days, especially with neurologic features — possible meningitis, especially in infants

causes.description: Salmonella colonizes the intestines and reproductive tracts of poultry and livestock without causing visible illness. Undercooked chicken, raw or undercooked eggs, and beef products are responsible for an estimated 40% of US foodborne salmonellosis cases.

Fresh produce — particularly leafy greens, melons, tomatoes, and sprouts — can be contaminated through irrigation water, animal contact, or handling. Sprouts are a high-risk category because the warm, humid growing conditions also promote bacterial growth.

causes.cause: Contact with reptiles, amphibians, and backyard poultry
causes.description: Up to 90% of reptiles harbor Salmonella asymptomatically. Touching turtles, lizards, snakes, frogs, or backyard chickens — including handling cages, water dishes, or the environment — is a documented exposure route especially in children. CDC outbreak data link 11% of cases to animal contact.

Inadequate handwashing after defecation allows transmission within households, daycare settings, and long-term care facilities. Asymptomatic stool shedding for weeks after recovery sustains household and institutional spread.

Untreated or inadequately treated drinking water, recreational water (lakes, rivers, splash pads), and well water can transmit Salmonella, particularly in resource-limited settings or after flooding events.

Salmonella from raw poultry, eggs, or meat contaminates utensils, cutting boards, and surfaces, then transfers to ready-to-eat foods. Standard food safety practices (separate cutting boards, hand washing, proper cooking temperatures) eliminate most household transmission.

riskFactors.description: Children under 5 have the highest US incidence (~64 per 100,000), driven by less mature immunity, hand-to-mouth behavior, and reptile or backyard poultry exposure.
Older adults have higher rates of severe disease, bacteremia, and focal complications including mycotic aneurysm. Comorbidity and reduced gastric acidity contribute.
HIV-infected adults have 20-100x higher rates of invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis. Recurrent bacteremia is a stage 4 HIV-defining condition.
Predisposes to Salmonella osteomyelitis, particularly of long bones. Functional asplenia and bone infarction provide a niche for hematogenous seeding.

riskFactors.factor: Proton pump inhibitor or H2 blocker use
riskFactors.description: Reduced gastric acidity lowers the infectious dose required for Salmonella to traverse the stomach. Observational data link PPI use to a 2-3 fold increased risk.

riskFactors.factor: Reptile, amphibian, or backyard poultry ownership
riskFactors.description: CDC documents 11% of cases tied to animal contact. Risk is highest in children under 5; CDC discourages reptile pets in homes with young children.
Travel to South Asia, Africa, and parts of Latin America carries higher risk due to food and water hygiene differences, plus exposure to multidrug-resistant strains.

riskFactors.factor: Antibiotic use in the prior month
riskFactors.description: Disruption of gut microbiota lowers colonization resistance, increasing the chance that ingested Salmonella will establish infection. The relative risk increases roughly 3-fold within 30 days of antibiotic exposure.

affectedDemographics: Highest rates in children under 5 (~64 per 100,000) and adults over 65
Sub-Saharan Africa: invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis is a leading cause of bloodstream infection in children, with case-fatality rates of 20-25% in some cohorts
Outbreak-associated cases account for roughly 5% of US incidence; sporadic foodborne exposure dominates
Multidrug-resistant strains now represent ~6% of US Salmonella isolates (NARMS surveillance)
diagnosisOverview: Most uncomplicated salmonellosis is diagnosed clinically and managed without confirmatory testing. Stool culture remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis and serotyping, recommended when the illness is severe, prolonged beyond 5-7 days, accompanied by high fever, bloody, or occurs in high-risk hosts (infants under 3 months, immunocompromised, sickle cell disease, healthcare workers, food handlers). Molecular gastrointestinal panels (multiplex PCR) detect Salmonella DNA from stool within hours and are now widely available in US hospitals; sensitivity exceeds 95%. A positive PCR is followed by reflex stool culture to obtain an isolate for serotyping and susceptibility testing, both essential for public health surveillance and antibiotic selection. Blood cultures are obtained in any patient with high fever (over 39°C), prolonged illness, or signs of invasive disease; bacteremia is detected in roughly 5% of NTS infections overall but in a much higher proportion of infants and immunocompromised hosts. Imaging — vertebral MRI, abdominal CT with contrast, transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiography — is added when focal complications are suspected. Mycotic aortic aneurysm should be specifically excluded in any older adult with Salmonella bacteremia, especially with new back, abdominal, or flank pain. Public health reporting is mandatory in all US states, and serotyping data feed into CDC PulseNet for outbreak detection. Differential diagnoses include other invasive bacterial enteritides (Campylobacter, Shigella, enteroinvasive E. coli), Clostridioides difficile in recent antibiotic users, viral gastroenteritis (norovirus, rotavirus), and inflammatory bowel disease flares. Travel history, animal exposure, and the food consumed in the 7 days before onset guide testing intensity and outbreak investigation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for diagnosis, isolation, serotyping, and antibiotic susceptibility testing. Provides public health surveillance data through PulseNet. Sensitivity 70-90% with a single sample; higher with serial cultures.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool sample collected at home or in clinic; transported in selective enrichment media. Results take 2-5 days for culture and identification.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid molecular detection of Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli pathotypes, and other gastroenteritis pathogens from a single stool specimen.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same stool collection process. Results return in 1-4 hours in most US hospital labs. Reflex stool culture is recommended for serotyping.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bacteremia in severe or prolonged illness, high fever, immunocompromised hosts, and infants. Standard practice in any patient with suspected invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets of blood cultures drawn from separate sites, ideally before antibiotics. Results positive in 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies osteomyelitis, mycotic aneurysm, endocarditis, or abscess in patients with invasive disease and focal signs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Targeted to the suspected site. MRI of the spine for back pain, CT abdomen with contrast for aortic concern, transesophageal echo for endocarditis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Guides therapy when antibiotics are indicated. Multidrug-resistant strains now represent ~6% of US Salmonella, including ciprofloxacin- and ceftriaxone-resistant isolates.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed automatically by the clinical microbiology lab on any positive isolate. Results in 1-2 days after culture identification.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses dehydration severity, electrolyte derangement, and inflammatory response. Leukocytosis with left shift is common in invasive disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within hours.

treatmentOverview: Most non-typhoidal salmonella gastroenteritis is self-limited and treated with supportive care alone — oral rehydration, antipyretics, and a return to normal diet as tolerated. Antibiotics are not routinely recommended for uncomplicated infection in immunocompetent adults because they prolong stool shedding by an average of 1-2 weeks (Onwuezobe 2012 Cochrane review) and select for resistance without shortening symptoms. The 2017 IDSA guideline on infectious diarrhea reserves antibiotics for severe disease (high fever, sepsis features, bloody diarrhea over 7 days), infants under 3 months, adults over 50 with atherosclerotic disease, immunocompromised hosts including HIV-positive patients with low CD4 counts, sickle cell disease, and patients with prosthetic vascular grafts or joints. First-line antibiotics for adults are fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily) or third-generation cephalosporins (ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV daily); duration is typically 7-14 days for uncomplicated bacteremia and 4-6 weeks for osteomyelitis, endocarditis, or mycotic aneurysm. Azithromycin 500 mg daily for 7 days is an alternative, particularly in regions with fluoroquinolone resistance. Empiric therapy choice should reflect local resistance patterns and current CDC NARMS data; susceptibility testing then refines the regimen. Children receive azithromycin or ceftriaxone preferentially over fluoroquinolones. Mycotic aortic aneurysm requires both prolonged antibiotic therapy and vascular surgical repair — antibiotics alone are inadequate due to ongoing graft seeding risk. Endocarditis warrants 4-6 weeks of intravenous bactericidal therapy and surgical valve assessment. Stool shedding can persist 4-6 weeks after recovery without antibiotics; food handlers, healthcare workers, and childcare staff may need negative follow-up stool cultures before returning to work per local public health rules. Reactive arthritis after salmonellosis is treated symptomatically with NSAIDs; antibiotics do not alter the post-infectious arthritis course. Reporting to local and state health departments is mandatory in all US states to enable outbreak detection.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral rehydration therapy with WHO/UNICEF ORS solution
medicalTreatments.description: Cornerstone of treatment for uncomplicated salmonellosis. Replaces fluid and electrolyte losses; oral route effective for most patients. Intravenous rehydration reserved for inability to tolerate oral fluids or severe dehydration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents over 95% of dehydration-related hospitalizations when used appropriately.

medicalTreatments.name: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO twice daily × 5-7 days (uncomplicated bacteremia, 7-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral antibiotic for severe or invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis in adults. Avoid in pregnancy and children under 18 (use ceftriaxone or azithromycin instead). Check local resistance — fluoroquinolone resistance is rising in South and Southeast Asia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in 85-95% of susceptible infections; resolution of bacteremia within 48-72 hours of effective therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV daily × 7-14 days (bacteremia); 4-6 weeks (focal infection)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line intravenous therapy for hospitalized patients with invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis, children, and pregnant patients. Excellent CNS penetration for meningitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in 90% of susceptible cases; resistance is rare in the US (under 3% of isolates).

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin 500 mg PO daily × 7 days (alternative)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in pregnancy, in children, when fluoroquinolone resistance is suspected (e.g., travel-associated cases from South Asia), and for stool shedding clearance in selected outbreaks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in 85-90% of susceptible cases; shorter duration than fluoroquinolones in head-to-head trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg PO twice daily × 7-14 days
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative oral agent when susceptibility is documented; first-line in some resource-limited settings due to low cost. Resistance has grown to over 25% of US isolates, limiting empiric use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of 80-90% in susceptible strains; not appropriate for empiric therapy without susceptibility data.

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical repair of mycotic aortic aneurysm
medicalTreatments.description: Required for any Salmonella-infected aortic aneurysm because antibiotics alone are inadequate. Endovascular or open repair combined with 6+ weeks of intravenous antibiotics, often followed by lifelong oral suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-day mortality 10-20% with combined therapy versus 60-100% with antibiotics alone (Hsu 2008 series).

surgicalOptions.name: Mycotic aneurysm repair (endovascular or open)
surgicalOptions.description: Mandatory for any Salmonella-infected aneurysm of the aorta, iliac, or peripheral artery. Combined with prolonged antibiotic therapy and often lifelong oral suppression.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day survival 80-90% in centers experienced with infected aortic disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement of osteomyelitis
surgicalOptions.description: Needed when imaging shows sequestrum, abscess, or hardware involvement. Particularly important in sickle cell disease with extensive bone necrosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic resolution in 80-90% when combined with 4-6 weeks of intravenous antibiotics.

surgicalOptions.name: Valve replacement for Salmonella endocarditis
surgicalOptions.description: Considered for large vegetations, embolic events, persistent bacteremia, or congestive heart failure. Performed alongside intravenous antibiotic therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Operative survival ~85% in case series; long-term survival depends on baseline cardiac function.

whySeeSpecialist: Infectious disease consultation is warranted in any case of invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis: bacteremia, focal infection, immunocompromised host, sickle cell disease, prosthetic device involvement, or treatment failure. Mycotic aneurysm requires combined infectious disease and vascular surgery input. Pediatric ID is involved for infants under 3 months and immunocompromised children.
doctorSelectionGuide: Most cases are managed by primary care or pediatrics. Choose a clinician familiar with the IDSA 2017 infectious diarrhea guideline who orders stool culture or PCR appropriately, avoids unnecessary antibiotics, and recognizes red flags for invasive disease. For invasive cases, look for an infectious disease physician with access to susceptibility testing and a multidisciplinary team for focal complications.
preventionStrategies: Cook poultry to internal temperature of 74°C (165°F) and ground beef to 71°C (160°F); use a meat thermometer
Avoid raw or undercooked eggs in foods such as homemade mayonnaise, hollandaise, eggnog, and unpasteurized desserts
Wash hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds after handling raw meat, poultry, eggs, or pets — alcohol gels are not adequate for visible contamination
Keep reptiles and amphibians out of homes with children under 5 years and immunocompromised adults; never bathe reptiles in kitchen sinks
Use separate cutting boards for raw meat versus produce, and disinfect surfaces and utensils after preparation
Choose pasteurized milk, juice, and egg products, especially during pregnancy and for high-risk household members
Wash all produce thoroughly under running water, including melons before cutting, even if the rind will be discarded
Vaccinate poultry flocks (commercial farms) and follow safe handling protocols at backyard poultry operations
lifestyleModifications: Drink plenty of fluids, especially oral rehydration solution, during the acute illness
Resume a normal diet as soon as tolerated — the BRAT diet is no longer recommended; balanced nutrition aids gut recovery
Avoid anti-motility agents such as loperamide in invasive disease or bloody diarrhea; they prolong illness and increase complication risk
Stay home from work, daycare, school, or healthcare settings until 48 hours after diarrhea resolves
Wash hands frequently during and for 1-2 weeks after illness to limit household and community spread
Food handlers, healthcare workers, and childcare staff may need negative follow-up stool cultures before returning per local public health rules

recommended: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) containing balanced glucose and electrolytes during active diarrhea
Bland easily-digested foods initially — bananas, rice, applesauce, toast, plain crackers — but progress to a normal diet within 1-2 days
Probiotics with documented strains (Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG, Saccharomyces boulardii) may modestly shorten illness duration
Yogurt with live cultures once tolerating dairy again
Soup, broths, and adequate plain water for fluid replacement
avoid: Raw or undercooked eggs, poultry, meat, and seafood until fully cooked
Unpasteurized milk, cheese, juice, and unboiled water from untrusted sources
Alcohol and caffeine during active illness — both worsen dehydration
Anti-motility drugs (loperamide) in fever or bloody diarrhea
Sugary sodas and fruit juices as primary rehydration — high osmotic load can worsen diarrhea

exerciseGuidelines: Rest during the acute febrile phase. Gradual return to normal activity once diarrhea, fever, and dehydration have resolved — typically within 1-2 weeks of symptom onset. Patients with reactive arthritis should avoid weight-bearing exercise on affected joints until inflammation subsides.
dailyManagement: Drink at least 2-3 liters of oral rehydration solution daily during active diarrhea, more in hot climates
Take temperature twice daily — persistent fever beyond 5-7 days suggests invasive disease
Wash hands thoroughly after every bowel movement and before handling food, especially when household contacts are present
Avoid preparing food for others until 48 hours after diarrhea resolves
Return for evaluation if symptoms worsen, dehydration develops, or new focal symptoms (back pain, joint pain) appear
prognosis: For immunocompetent adults and older children, prognosis is excellent — over 95% recover fully within 7-10 days without antibiotic therapy. Mortality from uncomplicated salmonellosis in the US is under 0.1%. Invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis carries higher mortality: 7-10% with treatment in healthy adults, up to 25% in HIV-positive adults in resource-limited settings, and 5-15% in infants. Mycotic aortic aneurysm carries the worst prognosis — 30-day mortality 60-100% without surgery, 10-20% with combined antibiotic and surgical management. Stool shedding persists for an average of 4-5 weeks after symptom resolution without antibiotics; chronic carriage beyond one year occurs in under 1% of non-typhoidal cases (more common with typhoidal strains and biliary disease). Reactive arthritis develops in 2-15% of cases, especially in HLA-B27 carriers, with most cases resolving within 6 months but a minority becoming chronic. The dominant determinants of poor outcome are age (extremes of life), immune status, sickle cell disease, presence of prosthetic vascular grafts or joints, and delay in recognizing focal complications.
recoveryTimeline: Diarrhea typically resolves over 4-7 days; fever within 2-3 days. Patients feel back to normal energy within 7-14 days. Stool shedding continues for an average of 4-5 weeks. Invasive disease requires longer treatment (2 weeks for uncomplicated bacteremia, 4-6 weeks for osteomyelitis and endocarditis) and full recovery may take 6-12 weeks. Reactive arthritis, when it occurs, peaks at 2-6 weeks and usually resolves over 3-6 months.
complications: Bacteremia and sepsis, occurring in 5% of NTS cases and a higher proportion of infants and immunocompromised patients
Mycotic aortic aneurysm — particularly in adults over 50 with atherosclerosis; presents with new back, abdominal, or flank pain
Salmonella osteomyelitis, especially of long bones in sickle cell disease patients
Reactive arthritis (Reiter syndrome) in 2-15% of cases, more common in HLA-B27 carriers
Septic arthritis, particularly involving knees and hips
Meningitis — predominantly in infants and immunocompromised adults
Chronic asymptomatic carriage with ongoing transmission risk, especially with biliary stones
Endocarditis — uncommon but high mortality if untreated

supportResources.name: CDC — Salmonella Resources
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education, outbreak alerts, and food safety information.

supportResources.name: FDA Food Safety
supportResources.description: Practical food handling and recall information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Infectious Gastroenteritis (other causes)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Norovirus, rotavirus, Campylobacter, Shigella, and E. coli all cause similar acute diarrheal illnesses. Salmonella tends to have a longer incubation (12-72 hours), more fever, and is linked to poultry, eggs, or reptile exposure. Stool PCR or culture confirms the specific pathogen.

confusedWithConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Typhoid fever, caused by S. Typhi or S. Paratyphi, is a systemic illness with prolonged fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, and constipation more commonly than diarrhea — distinct from non-typhoidal gastroenteritis. Blood culture is positive in typhoid; stool culture more often in non-typhoidal disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Shigellosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Shigella causes bloody diarrhea and tenesmus with a much smaller infectious dose, and is more often acquired through person-to-person spread than food. The illness is shorter (4-7 days) but more inflammatory. Stool culture or PCR distinguishes.

relatedConditions.name: Typhoid Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Same Salmonella genus but distinct serovars (Typhi, Paratyphi) and clinical syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Infectious Gastroenteritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Salmonella is one of the leading bacterial causes of acute gastroenteritis.

relatedConditions.name: Shigellosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares fecal-oral transmission and similar acute presentation; bundled stool testing distinguishes.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: Non-typhoidal salmonellosis with recurrent bacteremia is an AIDS-defining condition; co-infected patients have 20-100x higher invasive rates.

faqs.question: How long does salmonella infection last?
faqs.answer: Most salmonella infections cause diarrhea, fever, and abdominal cramping that resolve over 4-7 days without antibiotics. Stool shedding can continue for an average of 4-5 weeks after symptoms clear. Severe or invasive infections require 7-14 days of antibiotic treatment and longer recovery.

faqs.question: Do I need antibiotics for salmonella?
faqs.answer: Most immunocompetent adults with uncomplicated salmonella gastroenteritis do not need antibiotics — they prolong stool shedding without shortening illness. Antibiotics are recommended for infants under 3 months, adults over 50 with atherosclerotic disease, immunocompromised patients, sickle cell disease, high fever, sepsis features, or focal infections.

faqs.question: What foods commonly cause salmonella?
faqs.answer: Poultry, eggs, beef, and unpasteurized dairy are the most common food sources. Fresh produce, sprouts, melons, peanut butter, and even cereal have caused major outbreaks. Reptile and backyard poultry contact accounts for 11% of US cases per CDC tracking.

faqs.question: How is salmonella diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Stool culture is the gold standard and provides serotype and antibiotic susceptibility information. Multiplex PCR panels detect Salmonella DNA in stool within hours and are widely used. Blood cultures are added in severe or invasive disease. Diagnosis is largely clinical for mild outpatient cases.

faqs.question: How long after eating salmonella-contaminated food do symptoms start?
faqs.answer: Symptoms typically appear 6-72 hours after ingestion, most commonly 12-36 hours. Onset varies with the inoculum, host gastric acidity, and the specific serovar. Outbreaks often become clear when several people who ate the same meal develop similar symptoms within a 1-2 day window.

faqs.question: Is salmonella contagious between people?
faqs.answer: Yes. Salmonella spreads via the fecal-oral route — inadequate handwashing after defecation can transfer bacteria to food, surfaces, or directly between people. Household and daycare outbreaks are well documented. Wash hands thoroughly after every bowel movement during illness and for 1-2 weeks afterward.

faqs.question: How do I prevent salmonella?
faqs.answer: Cook poultry to 74°C (165°F) internal temperature, ground beef to 71°C (160°F), avoid raw eggs, wash hands and surfaces after handling raw meat, use separate cutting boards, keep reptiles away from young children and food preparation areas, and choose pasteurized dairy and juices.

faqs.question: Can salmonella cause long-term problems?
faqs.answer: Most cases resolve completely. A minority develop reactive arthritis 1-3 weeks after the gastrointestinal phase, particularly in people with HLA-B27. Invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis can cause osteomyelitis (especially in sickle cell disease) and mycotic aortic aneurysm in older adults. These complications require prolonged antibiotic and sometimes surgical treatment.

faqs.question: Can salmonella be deadly?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated salmonella gastroenteritis has US mortality under 0.1%. Invasive disease has higher mortality — 7-10% in healthy adults, up to 25% in HIV-positive adults in resource-limited settings. Untreated mycotic aortic aneurysm has near-100% mortality, reduced to 10-20% with combined surgery and antibiotics.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for non-typhoidal salmonella?
faqs.answer: No licensed vaccine exists for non-typhoidal salmonella as of 2026. Vaccines are in development, particularly targeting invasive serovars relevant to sub-Saharan African children. Typhoid-specific vaccines (for S. Typhi, not non-typhoidal) are widely available and recommended for travelers to endemic regions.

faqs.question: Can I get salmonella from my pet reptile?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to 90% of reptiles carry Salmonella asymptomatically. Touching the animal, its enclosure, or any surface it has contacted can transmit infection. CDC strongly advises against reptile pets in homes with children under 5, pregnant women, or immunocompromised people. Always wash hands thoroughly after any contact.

faqs.question: Does salmonella cause bloody stool?
faqs.answer: Visible blood in stool occurs in roughly 5-10% of salmonella cases, more often in children and in colitis-predominant illness. Significant bloody diarrhea may also signal Shigella, enterohemorrhagic E. coli, or Campylobacter and warrants stool testing and clinical evaluation rather than self-treatment.

faqs.question: Can salmonella be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. Uncomplicated infection self-resolves; invasive disease responds to appropriate antibiotics in over 90% of cases. Chronic asymptomatic carriage occurs in under 1% of non-typhoidal cases and may require prolonged antibiotic therapy or, rarely, cholecystectomy for biliary carriage.

faqs.question: Why are children more likely to get salmonella?
faqs.answer: Children under 5 have less mature immunity, lower gastric acidity, frequent hand-to-mouth behavior, and more frequent reptile and backyard poultry contact. They are also more susceptible to dehydration. CDC data show this age group has US incidence approximately 5 times the adult rate.

faqs.question: Should I take loperamide for salmonella diarrhea?
faqs.answer: Avoid loperamide and other anti-motility agents in salmonella with high fever, bloody stool, or systemic symptoms. They can prolong stool shedding, increase invasive disease risk, and worsen outcomes. Use oral rehydration as the primary symptom-management tool.

faqs.question: Can I work while I have salmonella?
faqs.answer: Stay home from work, daycare, school, or healthcare settings until 48 hours after diarrhea resolves. Food handlers, healthcare workers, and childcare staff may need negative follow-up stool cultures before returning per local public health rules. Continue diligent hand hygiene for 1-2 weeks afterward.

faqs.question: How is salmonella in sickle cell disease different?
faqs.answer: Sickle cell disease predisposes to Salmonella osteomyelitis of long bones — far more common than typical bacterial osteomyelitis in this population. Any febrile sickle cell patient with new bone pain should have blood cultures and bone imaging, with empiric coverage including antibiotics active against Salmonella.

faqs.question: Can salmonella come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Recurrence after appropriate treatment is uncommon in healthy hosts. Relapse rates of 10-25% occur in HIV-positive patients with low CD4 counts, sometimes requiring prolonged suppression. Chronic carriage in under 1% of non-typhoidal cases can intermittently shed bacteria for over a year.

faqs.question: How much does salmonella treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Most uncomplicated cases require only oral rehydration and over-the-counter medications, costing under USD 50. Hospital admission for severe disease or invasive infection costs USD 5,000-30,000 in the US depending on length of stay. Generic ciprofloxacin and azithromycin are inexpensive worldwide.

faqs.question: Can salmonella spread through water?
faqs.answer: Yes. Contaminated drinking water, well water, recreational lake or river water, and even some splash pads have caused outbreaks. Risk is higher in resource-limited settings, after flooding events, or with private well water that is not regularly tested. Boiling water for 1 minute kills Salmonella.

faqs.question: Is salmonella reportable to public health?
faqs.answer: Yes. Salmonellosis is a nationally notifiable disease in the US. Clinical laboratories and clinicians report cases to local and state health departments, which feed data into the CDC National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System and PulseNet for outbreak detection. Reporting drives the ability to identify food-source outbreaks.

metaDescription: Salmonella infection: foodborne bacterial illness from poultry, eggs, reptiles. Symptoms, stool testing, hydration, and when to use antibiotics reviewed.
